file_name: Wang et al. - 2024 - Artificial intelligence–aided diagnosis system for the detection and classification of private-part.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a 2-stage AI-aided diagnosis system for private-part skin diseases (PPSDs). The first stage used a multitask detection network (likely CNN-based, as referenced in the introduction regarding CNNs for skin disease diagnosis) to detect and classify skin lesions (type, color, shape). The second stage used a knowledge graph and decision network to classify seven specific PPSDs (condyloma acuminatum, Paget disease, eczema, pearly penile papules, genital herpes, syphilis, and Bowen disease). The system was evaluated on clinical images from 635 patients.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and a 'Decision Analytical Modeling Study'. It describes the development and evaluation of a novel 2-stage AI-aided diagnosis system using original data from 635 patients, including training a multitask detection network and conducting a reader study with dermatologists. The study presents new experimental results, metrics (precision, recall, F1-score), and a new dataset, which characterizes it as original research rather than a review.

title: Artificial Intelligence–Aided Diagnosis System for the Detection and Classification of Private-Part Skin Diseases: Decision Analytical Modeling Study
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated a two-stage artificial intelligence-aided diagnosis system for the detection and classification of private-part skin diseases (PPSDs). The system utilized a multitask detection network to identify skin lesion characteristics and a knowledge graph-based decision network to classify seven specific PPSDs, including condyloma acuminatum and Paget disease. The model was trained on 2,701 skin lesion samples from 520 patients at Institute 1 and tested on 115 clinical images from 115 patients at Institute 2. Evaluation included comparing the system's performance against existing advanced algorithms and assessing its impact on the diagnostic accuracy of 13 dermatologists in a reader study. The AI system achieved an average precision, recall, and F1-score of 0.81, 0.86, and 0.83, respectively, outperforming existing algorithms. Furthermore, the system improved the average F1-scores of junior, intermediate, and senior dermatologists by 16%, 7%, and 4%, demonstrating its potential to assist in early diagnosis and self-screening.

all_task_types.target: Seven private-part skin diseases (condyloma acuminatum, Paget disease, eczema, pearly penile papules, genital herpes, syphilis, and Bowen disease)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of PPSDs using a knowledge graph-based decision network. The system also performs multitask classification of skin lesion attributes (type, color, and shape) in the first stage.
Multitask detection network used to detect and localize skin lesions via bounding boxes in the first stage of the system.

datasets_used.name: Institute 1 Dataset (Training Cohort)
datasets_used.description: Retrospective collection of clinical images and corresponding medical records from Xiangya Hospital of Central South University (Institute 1) between July 2019 and April 2022. Used for system development and training. Contains 520 patients with 2701 skin lesion samples. Most cases had pathological confirmation; remaining cases were recognized by 3 dermatologists. Includes labels for bounding boxes, lesion type (papule, plaque, nodule, erosion, vesicle), color (red, brown, skin color), and shape (rice shape, round, irregular, papillary, cauliflower).

use.training: Used for training the multitask detection network (first stage) and developing the system. Provided 2701 cropped skin lesion images for multitask classification of type, color, and shape.
use.other: Used for data labeling by three dermatologists to simulate visual diagnosis process.

datasets_used.name: Institute 2 Dataset (Testing Cohort)
datasets_used.description: Retrospective collection of clinical images and corresponding medical records from Wuhan No.1 Hospital (Institute 2) between July 2019 and April 2022. Used for system evaluation. Contains 115 patients. Followed Helsinki protocol and good clinical practice guidelines. Inclusion criteria: private parts skin diseases, images do not affect judgment, informed consent holder. Exclusion criteria: inability to capture clear images, no consent.

use.testing: Used for testing the whole 2-stage AI-aided diagnosis system. Evaluated system performance (precision, recall, F1-score) and used in a reader study with 13 dermatologists to assess diagnostic enhancement.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the multitask classification model in various categories by observing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
To plot the true positive rate against the false positive rate at various threshold settings for skin lesion classification.
To measure the entire 2D area underneath the ROC curve, representing better classification performance for skin lesion type, color, and shape recognition.
To measure PPSD classification performance, defined as TP/(TP+FP).
To measure PPSD classification performance, defined as TP/(TP+FN).
To measure PPSD classification performance, defined as 2×precision×recall/(precision+recall), and to compare diagnostic performance of dermatologists with and without AI support.
To assess the impact of the AI system on diagnostic accuracy by comparing dermatologist performance in two scenarios: without system support (scenario 1) and with system support (scenario 2).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) and absolute differences (delta) without statistical significance testing.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage AI-aided diagnosis system consisting of a multitask detection network (first stage) and a knowledge graph-based decision network (second stage). The multitask detection network comprises a feature extraction module and a region proposal network (RPN).

stage_1_detection_network.function: Detects skin lesions (bounding boxes) and classifies their type (papule, plaque, nodule, erosion, vesicle), color (red, brown, skin color), and shape (rice shape, round, irregular, papillary, cauliflower).
stage_1_detection_network.reference: Detailed architecture in Section S1 of Multimedia Appendix 1.

stage_2_decision_network.details: Constructed by dermatologists referencing medical guidelines and professional textbooks. Composed of 30 decision paths. Encodes detection results from stage 1 and medical records into vectors using predefined encoding rules. Inference derived by multiplying elements within the vector. Probabilities of decision paths for the same disease are summed and normalized.
stage_2_decision_network.reference: Detailed inference process in Figure S2 and Section S2 of Multimedia Appendix 1.

training_details.dataset: 2701 skin lesion samples from 520 patients at Institute 1.
training_details.labels: Bounding boxes, lesion type, color, and shape.

classification_or_output_head.stage_1: Multitask classification head predicting type, color, and shape with confidence scores.
classification_or_output_head.stage_2: Knowledge graph-based decision network outputting final disease diagnosis probabilities for seven PPSDs (condyloma acuminatum, Paget disease, eczema, pearly penile papules, genital herpes, syphilis, and Bowen disease).

preprocessing_techniques: Bounding box annotation by dermatologists to label skin lesions
Cropping of skin lesion regions from original images based on bounding boxes
Data augmentation for erosion and vesicle lesion types by adding images from other body parts

data_augmentation_techniques.details: For the erosion and vesicle lesion types, which had relatively small amounts of data, images of these two types of skin lesions were added from other human body parts to balance the dataset.

conclusions: The study developed and evaluated the first AI-aided diagnosis system specifically for private-part skin diseases (PPSDs), utilizing a two-stage approach comprising a multitask detection network and a knowledge graph-based decision network.

The proposed system achieved an average precision of 0.81, recall of 0.86, and F1-score of 0.83 on the test cohort, outperforming seven existing advanced deep learning algorithms (including ResNet101, SENet, and Swin-twins).

The AI system significantly improved the diagnostic performance of dermatologists across all experience levels: junior dermatologists saw a 16% increase in average F1-score, intermediate dermatologists saw a 7% increase, and senior dermatologists saw a 4% increase.
Junior dermatologists demonstrated the greatest improvement in diagnostic metrics (precision, recall, and F1-score) when assisted by the AI system compared to intermediate and senior groups.

The system simulates the dermatological diagnostic process by detecting skin lesion characteristics (type, color, shape) and combining them with medical records via a knowledge graph, thereby reducing reliance on large-scale image data and mitigating issues related to complex backgrounds in clinical images.

The system has potential applications for patient self-screening to alleviate stigma and embarrassment associated with private-part diseases, as well as assisting dermatologists in early diagnosis and reducing diagnostic delays.
important_remarks: Limited external validation: The study relied on data from only two institutes (Xiangya Hospital and Wuhan No.1 Hospital), which may limit generalizability to other populations or clinical settings.
Small test cohort: The testing dataset consisted of only 115 patients from Institute 2, which is a relatively small sample size for evaluating diagnostic performance.

Poor performance on Bowen disease: The system achieved a low F1-score of 0.63 for Bowen disease, attributed to difficulties in distinguishing plaque shapes (irregular vs. round) and challenges in data labeling.

AUROC calculation limitation: The AUROC metric could not be properly calculated for certain diseases because the knowledge graph-based decision network resulted in zero confidence scores for some cases, as a single image cannot fit all decision paths.
Restricted disease scope: The system was designed to classify only seven specific private-part skin diseases (PPSDs), excluding other potential conditions.

Data scarcity and augmentation: The study acknowledged the difficulty of collecting PPSD data; for erosion and vesicle lesions, data augmentation was performed by adding images from other body parts, which may introduce bias.
No statistical significance testing: The paper reported absolute improvements in F1-scores for dermatologists but did not report p-values or statistical significance tests for these comparisons.

Purpose clarification: The authors explicitly state the system is intended to assist with self-screening and alleviate stigma, not to replace dermatologists, highlighting its role as an aid rather than a definitive diagnostic tool.
